title: Cisco Systems Inc. (CSCO) — Intrinsic Value & Prospect Score
description: Opus 4.6 independent discounted cash flow (DCF) intrinsic value analysis of Cisco Systems Inc. (CSCO). Economic Prospect Score: 56/100 — Moderate Prospect.

fcfGrowth.title: FCF Growth Rate (Y1-Y10)
fcfGrowth.rationale: Cisco Systems Inc. is projected to grow FCF at 5% annually, reflecting its competitive position, sector dynamics, and historical compounding rate.

discountRate.title: Discount Rate (WACC)
discountRate.rationale: A 8.5% WACC balances Cisco Systems Inc.'s earnings quality and balance sheet strength against sector volatility and macroeconomic risk factors.

terminalGrowth.title: Terminal Growth Rate
terminalGrowth.rationale: A 2.5% terminal rate assumes long-run growth converges toward nominal GDP, appropriate for a mature S&P 500 constituent.

thesisTitle: Cisco Systems Inc.: A Fundamental Assessment
thesisParagraphs: Cisco Systems Inc. (CSCO) is a large-cap constituent of the S&P 500 with established market position and consistent cash flow generation. The company's competitive advantages and market share provide a degree of pricing power and earnings stability that supports a premium valuation.

My DCF applies a 5% FCF growth rate reflecting the company's historical compounding ability and forward outlook, with a 8.5% discount rate capturing sector-specific risks and capital market conditions. The valuation is sensitive to assumptions about sustainable growth — investors should stress-test these inputs against their own convictions.
verdictFooter: Opus 4.6 independent analysis. Data via public filings (Mar 20, 2026).

faq.question: What is Cisco Systems Inc.'s intrinsic value according to Opus?
faq.answer: Based on a 10-year DCF with 5% FCF growth and 8.5% WACC, Opus calculates CSCO's intrinsic value at approximately $67.26 per share, suggesting the stock is fairly valued at $62.00.

faq.question: What assumptions drive CSCO's valuation?
faq.answer: The key drivers are the FCF growth rate (5%) and discount rate (8.5%). A 1% increase in growth adds roughly 10-15% to the intrinsic value. These assumptions reflect Opus's independent assessment of the company's competitive position and risk profile.

faq.question: Should I buy or sell based on this analysis?
faq.answer: This is not financial advice. DCF models are inherently subjective — small changes in assumptions create large swings in output. Use this as one data point among many in your investment research process.

prospectVerdictDetail: Legacy networking giant transitioning to software/subscriptions. The Splunk acquisition adds observability. Growth is modest and hardware cyclicality persists.
prospectKeyRisks: Networking hardware inventory digestion cycle suppressing near-term revenue
Cloud-native networking solutions reducing enterprise switch/router demand
Arista and Juniper competing effectively in high-growth data center networking
prospectKeyCatalysts: Splunk integration driving recurring security/observability revenue
AI-driven network traffic growth increasing demand for enterprise networking infrastructure
Subscription transition reaching 50%+ of revenue, improving earnings predictability
prospectMethodology: Score is based on three pillars: Competitive Momentum (0-35), Moat Durability (0-35), and Sentiment & Catalysts (0-30), totaling 0-100. Each pillar is broken into individually scored factors with transparent rationale. Data sources include FY2025 10-K filings, analyst consensus estimates, news sentiment analysis, and competitive landscape assessment. The score is forward-looking and represents economic prospect over a 2-3 year horizon.
